We were talking about trying to figure out why and I think it's because I'm left-handed and Andre's right-handed. So if you'll notice in that image Andre has his beats, the beats outline is on the far left and then the documents follow after that one for each day. When I write, and this, this wasn't, I wasn't even aware of this until we just talked about this earlier today. When I write, my outline is on the far right. And then I, so I look over to the right and then I'm writing on the left. Andre doesn't reverse. I suspect that the obvious answer there is it's handedness. I'm left-handed, he's right-handed. So if you look at this and it feels a little weird to you, or when you set this up on your own screen you're like, that seems really weird. If you're left-handed, just try moving the outline to the right of the screen or to the right of the document that you're working on, whatever it is, but I think looking right and writing left, I think this is a handedness thing. So it's kind of weird, weird thing to be drawing attention to, but I feel, when I look at that, I feel my stress levels going through the roof. So I wrote a couple of things on the white board here about, you know, the conversation we had before we clicked record was, it really about where do we think the quicksand is and how do we think we can add the most value, just the nuance in this call that is not in the content and we know the content's dense. I think it might be worth reading through it a couple of times, just thinking out loud here, there's just a lot there and there's, it, it's, there's a lot there in pieces, but there's a lot there when you kind of roll it up in aggregate too, that this, this is a, there's a conceptual idea here, which is to slow down and to build a human relationship with another person before you rush ahead to, to, to, to an exchange of value. And when I say an exchange of value in that context, yes, building the relationship is inherently an exchange of value, of course, but I'm, when I say the exchange of value, I mean, when you sell something at some point, rather than rushing to that, we're slowing down, we're building the relationship first, we believe in the data we've seen over and over and over again, we believe that this, this has a profound difference on overall conversion rates, it has a profound difference on customer happiness, it has a profound distance, different, a profound impact on the long-term value of your efforts because you get, even if you're turning people away, you are not going to go through those, in this case, three days. The quality of who arrives at the other side is so much higher that it's just, it changes everything. This is, this really is a, this really is a practice that changes absolutely everything if you do it right. And don't rush through this. Don't, don't feel like this is a box you have to check where like, okay, I gotta, I gotta be nice and I gotta tell a few stories and whatever, you know, let's go, I want to get to the sale. It's not, don't do that. Like this is really an opportunity to connect with another human being who's reading your work and who's engaging with you and, and has raised his or her hand and actively taken a step toward you. They're saying, hey, what you've shown me so far is interesting and interesting enough to let you into my inbox. And once you're there, it's, it's, I think so many people feel so jaded because this has been abused so many times, in so many ways that there's, there's almost an expectation that's like, okay, they've opted in and now here it comes. I'm going to get the 25 emails and, you know, promo, promo, promo. And when you don't do that, it's, it's such a pleasant relief and it's so different that you stand out because everybody's shouting louder right now. You're not going to stand out by shouting louder. So it's the quiet voice now. It's the calm voice, the slower process that actually stands out because it's so different. We as a, as creatures sense contrast. And if everybody in the marketplace is hype, hype, hype, buy, buy, buy, and you're the person who's slow taking your time establishing the relationship, by definition, you stand out because you're different. I'll let you, let you riff on how easy it is to overthink and then what happens when we do. Yeah. So, I think, let me see how to, how to approach this because, you know, there's nothing good about overthinking something because it creates overwhelm and, and anxiety and, you know, your stress levels go up and then it's, then you just, you know, there's, there's nothing nice about that. And then there's some people that just mainly, you know, short enough, afforded to this category is we're not the type of people war or, or our brains aren't, aren't wired in a way that we can over figure stuff out. You know, we have to try to figure out every single beat, every single piece of the structure and understand all of that before we start writing because for us, we, we, we learn what we think on the page. So for us, so long as our, our beats or our, and our structure is, is loosely there, like very loosely there. Then we're good to go. So we would then go to email number one and start writing. Some people need to have a, have a subject line just to kind of like get them going, put, then put one there, if not, just leave it blank and it'll reveal itself along the way. And then you must just start writing. It's kind of like free writing in the sense that many, many, many times I don't know what to write because it, because it just doesn't show itself to me. It's just, I'm not quite sure what it is, what the angle is, what the hook is going to be, just to mainly the actual lead in. It's like, what's a good lead in for this email? And I just don't know what it is. I can, you know, I've, I've got my beats, I've got my structure, I've got some, I've got everything I need, but it's, it's just not there yet. And when that happens, which is fairly regular, I'll just start to write. And I know that it will reveal itself to me while I'm writing. And if I have to write an entire email, just to find out, find that one little thing and then delete everything and start again, then it's, then it's worth it. But if you don't start, you never get there. And then, and then you never finish. Presumably you've, you kind of similar, Sean? Yeah, it's interesting. You know, if you're trying to make sure that everything's perfect before you sit down to write, you're never going to write. It's just, it's too hard. But I do think there are some people who probably write really well from an outline. So if that's you, I write, I think the way I write is probably very strange. And, and it's in a sense. Other times I can't, it's everything's a knife fight. I can't write every sentence as a struggle. You know, sometimes I use an outline and it works. Other times I write an outline after I've written the work to, to check my work, because the, it was just all in my head. And so it's, I don't know, for everybody listening, I think, I think be aware that you're going to have your unique things that work for you. Everyone who writes has that toolkit of how we can, in the biggest one, the sledgehammer in the toolbox is procrastination. And procrastination has lots of different flavors. But the, and it's, it's funny, the easiest way to beat procrastination. It's the most difficult hurdle we face yet. It's the easiest one to get around. It's just the moment we do it. It takes so, it takes so much energy to make the choice to beat procrastination when we're writing. But the way to do it is, is exactly what Andre just described. You just have to sit down and start writing. And once you do that, that's, you'll gain momentum. So don't, when we started just thinking about over, talking about overthinking, overthinking is, it comes in a lot of forms. One, it's, oh, I have to understand this better. You know, maybe I should buy ARM, maybe I should buy a sphere of influence, maybe I, you know, and it's like, okay, that's, that's what Steven Pressfield calls the resistance, right? That's, that's the little voice in your head saying, Hey, don't write yet, because once you write, you're putting yourself out in the world. So maybe you need to study more. I know that voice. Well, I do that. I do that a lot. So I, in another way that overthinking comes up is, I don't, you know, I don't know what I'm going to say over three, all three emails. I don't know where the open loops are going to be and where I'm going to close up. Great. You will, all of that will reveal itself in the writing. And you don't, you know, there's nothing required other than to just sit down and write, right? You know, get your beats, line those up as best you can. If you're, if you're like me, you'll probably only, you'll only capture half of the beats in the beat sheet when you first get started. And you'll think you have them all like, yep, this is everything I need. And then when you look at what you write, you'll realize that you filled in 25, 50% more around that, that just got you started. If you're a very outline driven writer, you may find that you need to really refine the beats and put them in, you know, bulleted, indented, organized. Yeah, I think one of the things that may not stress some people out, but it could add tension when writing this is like, Oh, I don't know this. They don't even worry about that at this moment. And even open loops, you can write all three emails and then go back afterwards and figure out places where you would like to insert things to pull people forward at certain places. When I'm, there's emails for me seems very comfortable, which is, which is no surprise that are created or respond to madness before any, any of the other courses, even though I was, I was doing that, that stuff together, rather than, you know, many years earlier. But I've just, I've always felt comfortable in an email, like where it's, where it's just a text document. It's just me and, and, and a text page. But if I'm thinking about, Oh, I've got a right, like a sales letter in, in air quotes, a sales letter, or an ad, that stresses me out more because it's, it just feels different or a different environment. So, so the trick I've used to hack what, what's like a hack that I've used to trick my brain into thinking that I'm actually writing an email is I'll write everything in, in the same text editor that I use to write emails. If it's an ad, if it's a web page, if it's a article, if it's an essay, anything. And I tell myself at the time I'm writing an email. So every single piece of writing that I do, almost every single piece of writing I'm doing, I'm actually telling myself I'm writing an email. Because then it just comes up more naturally, more conversationally, there's, there's less pressure and there's less tension on myself. And then afterwards, I'll then like label it, this is an article. So yeah, I've, I've tricked you, Andre, you idiot. It's someday what we should do, like an entire checklist, like all the things we do to trick ourselves to do, like, because it's, it's comic. I mean, we do in it's, it's, you know, it's that idea of parking on a downhill slope. All of these things help us lean a little bit more towards the thing that we want and a little farther away from the stuff that we don't. So everybody has their own, you can't copy our, I mean, some of them may work, but it's hard to copy ours because it's really unique. They emerge from doing the actual work. And don't try to figure those out in the course of this workshop and try to make your own checklist yet. Just pay attention to it. Like what, what kind of stuff gets in your way? One thing about that you just mentioned about open loops that I want to just maybe think of something. When I, when I imagine that these three and a relationship building emails, I'm seeing in my head the, the graphic at the bottom of the screen with the four documents open. When I'm, when I think about that, there are two things that come to mind to share. One is we're seeing these from a 10,000 foot view. We're seeing the entire, we're seeing the ad, the landing page, all six emails and the offer. We're seeing it from that sort of God's eye view. It's all laid out in front of us and, and we know how everything connects and we know the path through it and we know where we're opening loops and closing them and when we close, we know all of that because our view is that, that omniscient all knowing, all seeing view, but the prospect is going through that process on the ground with a flashlight and his or her hand and the journey for them is evolving, you know, one word at a time, one piece at a time. So that view is very different. And when you, when you adopt that, when you, when you hold those two views in your mind simultaneously, it, you, you understand, you have this incredible advantage of seeing the whole thing, but it also orients you to the point of view of the person who's going through it, that they're seeing it open, really one sentence, one paragraph at a time over the course of all of that writing. So just keep that, the two perspectives in mind and then getting this point about open loops. I think you and I have honed our skill at the open loop just through practice for a long time, but they tend to show up for us just naturally. But I think until that happens, a way to train it for a way to think about it is if you imagine those three documents are open on your screen, I like to, at the high level, I like to think about for each one of those, what is the primary value that I want to give in this email? You know, what's like, what's the thing? This could be the thesis statement, it could be a controlling idea, but yes, there each of those emails is within a much larger thematic arc. And there's sort of a micro arc within the relationship building emails around a building relationship versus selling things. So there's, there's an arc within an arc. But within the, within those three emails, I just for just to get me through directionally, right, I like to know that, you know, this email is about X, like the value I want the, the person reading it to receive is X, here's a controlling idea for this, for email one, for email two, and for email three, each of them may be a little bit different, or they might all be together in some way, whatever it is. But here's the theme for each of them. That helps me write each of them. But the other thing that it does is now that I know what the theme is for each, and I know, because of that, some of the things I'll be talking about in each, it becomes very easy and very natural to open loops to the next day's email or the third day's email or whatever it is, because I know, you know, tomorrow, if today is about X, X and tomorrow's about Y, and the day three is about Z, I know when I'm talking about, when I'm writing the first email, I can, I can project forward for what's to come from the perspective of value for them. So if I know, for example, I'm just going to make this top of my head. I'm writing an email about writing, and I know that, you know, today's email is to orient to the high level, and then tomorrow I'm going to get into the mechanics of creating a beat sheet, and then the next day I'm going to get into the, the, how do you create an outline from beats, right? That, those are my three, three themes for the emails. When I'm writing day one, at some point, I'll mention what's coming in day two and day three, and just, I'll, that's, and that's just a, it's a great open loop. You know, I can just say, you know, and it can be something as explicit as, you know, today's email is going to be, it could be a 10,000 foot view of the way I think about the writing process. Tomorrow we'll cover creating beats and why they're important, and in day three we'll talk about how to arrange those beats and outline, right? Well, I've, that's just created some tension. Those aren't like, those aren't, you know, narrative open loops, it's just an outline of what's to come, but I've directed the prospect's attention to the next two days, too. So that's a very overt way to do it. And then if I'm, I know for me, when I write, if I'm writing about one thing and I'm thinking about something else, so if I'm writing about the overall outline and I get onto the topic of beats, I feel some tension that I want to explain it, but I know I'm explaining it in tomorrow's email. So what I might do is I might, I might mention it, I might give the overview, and then I might note like parenthetically something like, you know, I don't want to get too far ahead of myself because tomorrow's email is going to be a much deeper dive into creating beats, and we'll, I'll cover the details then. That's not a trick. Like I'm not, I'm not trying to trick somebody. It's just, it's me recognizing like I'm feeling some tension. Like I want to tell you more about this right now, but it's, and I'm telling myself and you that, like I'm relieving a little pressure for myself, creating some pressure for you, because I'm essentially saying, you know, I'm going, there's this thing I really want to do. It's, it's, it sort of fits right here, but I need more time to do it. I need to explain it in more detail. So tomorrow's email is devoted to that and we'll get to it then. That's, that creates tension. That's, that's really what this is all about. It's not, it's not trickery. This is actually how we, this is how we communicate as a species. I don't know, I just went off the reservation on that one. All that came from. Yeah. And, and you're taking somebody on a journey. So as long as it's interesting and it's pulling someone forward because it's something that's interesting to them, something that they want to learn, something that they want to know, it's going to relieve a problem. It's going to help solve, you know, sorry about the noise. It sounds noisy out there. Such a portion in your garage. Do your parents call them your garage, right? Isn't that, the Europeans call them? I don't know. Maybe. We're really off the reservation. Just before I lose the thought, something that just popped into my head. I was imagining as you were just talking that, you know, if somebody could see the entire, all of the writing in one shot, like, how would they feel about it? Like if they could, if we sat them down and said, here, instead of, instead of it being an ad to a landing page to six emails, if we wrote it as one long narrative, like how would that feel? And the thought that popped into my head was it wouldn't feel as good. And the reason this is something I think to draw attention to, to the, really it's the space between the ad and the landing page and then the pause, the 24 hour pause between each of the emails. It reminds me of that the idea that the, that music is the space between the notes. And we think of music as like the notes, the sound, but it's the space between the notes that makes, is that's just as important for music. So the space between our writing when we get into email is just as important. It's, that's, that's the incubator of tension as you have, you, like if you imagine the example like eight before, and you've told someone what's going to happen over the next three days, and you've made that day two and day three seem really interesting. And you're pointing to another thing that's out on the horizon, which is the, that's really the, the value that your, your product or your service or offer or solves. Like that's the really big open loop and that you're pointing that down the road a little bit farther. Like the pause between the emails is where all the tension gets created. And that's where that's where the desire gets amplified. It's like, why there's this thing. And I know there are a couple, I know what's coming in the next couple of days, but it's all pointing to something even bigger. Like because they have to pause, it sounds counterintuitive, but that's actually working in your favor. I don't know where that, I don't know. I've never thought of that before. It's weird. There's, there's, there's something magical that happens when you write an email series. And, you know, maybe a, maybe a way to think about it is, and not to, for it to sound more complicated than it needs to be. But if you were, if, if you were going to be engaged in a, in a Netflix series and the series is three series long, or seven or whatever, you're not going to listen to the last one. And if you did, none of the ones previously would feel worth watching. You would, like everything's gone. There's, there's no, it's just, it's flat, right? And it's because that journey starts on Episode 1, Email 1, and Number 2 needs to be there for Number 3 to, to work and to get its power from. And, you know, someone reading an email, they're getting this, this enjoyment. They're not thinking it, thinking of it as enjoyment. They just, they're just feeling tension and they're feeling excitement and they're feeling anticipation and all those things. They're feeling those emotions, but it wouldn't work if it was just a long, like one long piece, because human nature is to skim, they'll read little bits here and there, and they won't have the, have the whole impact. But you get the whole impact when you, when you do it this way. And then by the time someone reads Email 3 or Email 6 or whatever it is, everything is different because of that journey that they've been through that you've engineered for them, you know, because you're, you're doing this in service of somebody else and you would be doing them a disservice if you just gave them Email 3 at the gate, because it just wouldn't be the same. Here we go. Here's, here's the last series or here's the last page, page of the book. Why don't you start there? Here's how the hero dies. What? And it's, this is, this is, I'm glad we, we went down this road. I don't, we've never really talked about this before. I don't know if I've ever important related my thoughts about this, but this, this actually points to two things that I think are interesting. I talked about some of them in the ad review. And now I better understand why I, like, I had the feeling that I did about one of the ads we discussed, but the, when we give, we give away the punchline too soon, then we've, we've taken out that excitement and that tension. Like we, we know that there's, there's, it's a dragon or whatever it is too early. Like we, you know, instead of knowing there's something coming and it's exciting and what's going on and they're like, Oh wow, it's a dragon or it's an army or whatever it is. Like when you kind of know it, then you, in your mind, you're, you're, you're planning for it and you're, then, then you're shifting away. The surprise is gone. So I think it's, it's, that's where we have to be really cautious is we don't want to, this feeds into through my next thoughts, contributes to the next thought, which is one of the things that we talk about, that we talked about in this module is that, and this is true for Facebook ad for the landing page and for the relationship building emails. And it's, it's more important than the relationship building emails because now we're, we're, what we think we're in as a three part sequence and then another three part sequence as we've envisioned these, these six emails, but we're really in a six part email sequence. And we're just the, the focus of the first three is not to try to get to the sale. It's to build a relationship out of respect for the other person, leading eventually to a way that we can contribute value to their lives in the right context for the right person. Through the exchange of monetary value for a product or service, something that we offer. But the trick now, trick is the worst word, but the magic trick here is that we can actually open loops to the product that are resolved in emails four or five and say, you're not the product you offer. As long as we don't, as long as we're not too transparent that it's, we're going to sell something later. So here, here's an example that popped into my mind as you were just talking Andre, that if we think about the example that I use, it's a three, if the over three emails, I'm going to email number one, I'm going to talk about in general how my writing process evolved day two, it's going to be the importance of creating beats and how to do that. And then day three is how to arrange the beats into a coherent outline. That's a, those are three, those are three emails where I can really build a relationship with somebody really contribute value to them, treat them like a customer before any money has changed hands and would really teach them something value. But if my, if what I'm going to offer an email four or five and six is my, my writing course, my, you know, how to use beats to write emails course, then, and I know that course has through like the, the outline of how to use beats to write emails. When I'm, when I'm in email, probably two and three, I could probably pull it off and email one, two as well. But I think emails two and three, I want to start opening loops to this idea of the value that the offer creates. Because these are not, these are not three independent things or two different campaigns in one big campaign. So what I want to be able to say is something like, in that first email, I might say, okay, over the next three emails, we're going to, in today's email, I'm going to explain the, you know, sort of how I think about writing and, and whatever my, you know, how, like, how I, how I, you know, how I came to write the way that I do and the stakes I made along the way or whatever and, and what I've learned tomorrow, I'm going to focus primarily on this idea of a story beat and, and how I use, and how to create one. And then day three, I'm going to explain how to put all of those into an outline to help your writing. When I get into day two, now I've, I can look out a little bit farther ahead. And what I know is in day four, the value that I'm going to offer is a systematic way to do that. So in day two, I might say something like, you know, yesterday we come, you know, you went through the broad overview. Today we're going to go in depth on what, what a story beat is and how to create them so that they're, you know, to help your writing. Tomorrow we'll talk about arranging them into a coherent outline. And later, and I might say, tomorrow we'll go into, you know, tomorrow we'll talk about how to use those no coherent outline. And later I'm going to explain how you, you, how you use that outline to develop, to create a process to produce better email or I'm getting a warning on that. And then later I'll explain, and then you just fill in whatever the value is of the product that you're going to sell that you're going to offer you're going to make on day four. So now I'm continuing to be like three, my attention is continuing to be three days ahead. But I have not yet said that there's anything for sale. I don't need to, I'm being completely honest. Day one is, is the overview day two is beats. Day three is the outline day four is when I reveal that there's actually a course and what is the primary value that that course provides? Well, that course shows somebody how to use, and to be clear, this is not a real course, I'm making this up, but the course is the how to use beats to create better emails or how to use the beat plus outline method to create better emails. Okay. Like I know that. So on day two and day three, I can be talking about the value they'll get from the course, but not mentioned that there's going to be something for sale. That is a very seamless transition. I'm not lying and saying, I'm going to get seven days of free value than on day three, I think, or day four, I pull something away. I'm simply looking out, consistently looking out three emails to keep them engaged in what's coming next over the next three days. And when it comes into the point, when it overlaps with the product focus, the offer focused emails, well, I just need, instead of focusing on the focusing on the product, I want to focus on the value that the product will, that I'm going to reveal is going to create for them. So by the time I reveal it, they're already interested in it, because I've been talking about why that thing that I'm going to reveal is valuable to them before they even know it exists. I hope that makes sense. It makes sense in my head. Right. So to add an extra layer to what Sean's just said, but again, without this, I'm not trying to complicate this. It's meant to make it easier, but we've just given you different ways to think about this. So in Sean's method, you, not method, where you know what's going to come down the line so you can queue up, you know, story elements or mention things that are going to come down the line. So there's another way of doing something similar is by telling stories that are more, where the message that's going to be solved later on, this thing that's in the story. So the lesson is in this little story. So it's more subtle and implied, but it does a similar thing. And in some ways it can be more powerful and it certainly can be more engaging because it's a story. It's not just a marketing thing. It's a fun, it's weird as I'm talking about this, thinking of a friend who does this really well, Nick Stevenson, he's a UK, he helps writers write and he helps writers market better. If you go to Tyler Nick Stevenson into Google, I think his site is first, your first 10,000 readers or something like that. Get onto his email list. And almost every single email that I've seen of his, there's a story about him and his family and his kids or whatever. He's moving home, he's doing something, but it always ends in the marketing message. But it's been told through the lens of something that's happened in his life. And he's normally always got a picture of him with the kid or the kid by itself or some mess, like wreckage that they've, the kids have just finished playing and then there's just like all this stuff there. But it's always engaging and interesting. But you can, you can put the marketing message in a story like that. And then it still does the same thing. It's putting someone forward or one of the things I used to do is when I used to help, I was living up in Spain and I was helping a friend become a marketer. So during, during that period, I had so many stories to tell about me helping him. So instead of me talking to somebody about, well, I've been on this journey and this is what I think you should do. I can tell it through the lens of me helping somebody else. And this is what's happened. And it's more their story, not my story now, but it's the same message. And in many ways it's more powerful because of now it's just on me saying, Hey, this is what you should do. I don't want to keep going around and around in a circle here, but you know, there's, there's different ways to do this. And if you've got interesting stories, then tell them in, in this, in this light. Yeah, I think Frank versus Matt is probably one of the best examples of that. I mean, you get invested into characters, two fictional characters, but they each represent very different types of people and the real people, not individuals, but an aggregate, they represent collections of people. And I remember, you know, forever ago, my first exposure, Frank and Matt as these two personas and like telling their story over time, you know, you get, you get caught up in it and you start your rooting for one of them. You know, you're, you want to be like one of them and you see yourself in both and you often see yourself a lot more in the, in the Frank character and you, and you want to be more like Matt. And there's just such a powerful, such a powerful vehicle. I think I'm not, I mean, you and I, we have, our writing backgrounds are different. You know, my background first was academic and then was journalistic. And that in yours, you have much more of a fiction and a narrative background than I do. So I think I tend to lean more toward just the facts. Like, here we go. And you have so much more texture and nuance in the narrative than I have. So they, the, I think the message for everybody here is don't, don't try to be the writer that you're not being the writer who you are. And it doesn't mean you can't, like I want to write far better narrative. And I want to push myself in that direction. But I don't, when I sit down to write and what I'm doing, you know, if I, if I do, if I need to do this exercise, for example, I, this weekend I needed to write these three relationship building emails, I might, I might try to use more of a narrative story driven approach. And I've done this enough times to know what it feels like when I do that. But at some point, I think what I would realize is like, if I'm, if I'm going to do this and I want to get it done in a week, weekend, whatever it is, I want to write like I write and I'll, and I'll get a better result. And, but still maybe add, maybe adds a little more nuance to it. So I, I mentioned that only not to contrast your method or your approach, my approach, or from the inclinations, but more for people who are listening to this, if you're like, I'd really love to be able to integrate story more and dialogue. And then you sit down and you feel like you're just all thumbs, but you have this other idea pulling at you, just run with what, what works for you. All right. And conversely, if you feel like you have to sit down and have those sort of architecture of, of pulling people forward, and it's not making sense, but their stories you want to tell on your dialogue that you remember, like, just do that, like do your thing, like be, be you and all of this. But that's, maybe that's the most important message that we can wrap up on is our, what we thought would be 15 minutes is probably going to be an hour recording, maybe both of our takes on the same thing, which is do you really well as a writer? And the, the, that's critically important in the, these first three emails where the primary goal is to build a relationship with somebody, be willing to be human, be willing to be yourself. And a lot of that is, is talk like you, write like you, communicate like you. So if I read, if I, if I read your three emails, and then met you in person, that it should feel like the same experience, the way you say things. Like, I know for me, I tend to write things parenthetically, because I think parenthetically and I talk parenthetically. So I'll be talking about one thing and then a thought will pop into my head, and I'll mention that thought and I'll continue on with the first thought. That's, if we hang out for any amount of time, you'll pick up on that in my communication very quickly. So when I write, I don't edit that out of my, I don't try to come back and think, well, that was a little disjointed, but we fix it. That's, that's how I communicate. So you'll have your own things. Just do you well, just, just and have fun with it. All right. And you can add to that thought on there before we wrap up today's short call that turned into a not short call. Yeah, I think we're at about 45 minutes now. So my last thought is one final tip for some people. This won't be for most people. And I think it's, it's good to get into the practice of writing like we've, like you've seen here. I'm in a writing group and one of the writers, it's, in fact, she's the only person that I know that does this. But her production is more than anybody I know. She writes about 5,000 words a day as a professional writer, but she doesn't top a single word. She, one of the, the, the things you'll always hear writers say is, don't edit, we've got it in the lesson. Don't, don't edit, right? You're, you're, you're first run through, just run through, turn off the spelling, turn off the grammar and just, and just write. And then once you've got your first shitty draft done, it's worth reading the, the, the article that we linked to when we mentioned shitty draft. Well, what she does is because she, she dictates into her, her iPhone, and then she gets a transcribed afterwards, you can't go back, you can't edit voice. So she just talks and she'll, and that's how she gets her 5,000 words every single day. You can't stumble like, like I do. I mean, I could never do, do audio. It's just, I would spend my life editing, but for somebody that's, that can think more clearly in the moment and can articulate it like Sean can, you know, he could probably get away with just talking to himself or talking to his phone and then hitting the, the stop button 10 minutes later. And there we go. He's got 2,000 words that he can then just sculpt and whittle down and, and, and chopping pieces and ta-da, you've got yourself your, your, your three emails. And then you can start working with it on the page now and messing about with it and adding your open loops and blah, blah, blah. But at least you've got the bulk of it out and it's all through just talking and then getting it transcribed with utter or loom or whatever, you know, audio app you're using. So I think that would be my last comment. Go right. Myself because I was, I was laughing that you think that you think I could possibly do that. Like it's hysterical. I think I, I, I have no, the idea of, and I've tried this a million times with the idea of sitting down and speaking coffee and then just editing it later. I've tried it and it is so horrifying to me. Like it doesn't, because my thinking is so imprecise and I'm just getting thoughts out and getting thoughts out. And then what I write is where the precision is and it's so, it's so funny to have you say that because I, like, I'm sweating thinking about that and like horrified if I had to write that way. I just, I couldn't do it. So, all right. All right. That's, we'll call that a wrap for today. Thank you. Thank you all for your attention and any questions of course drop them in the comments and get ready for week five. Cool. It's been good.
